meta.title: 4.12 Prompt Architecture as a Discipline
meta.description: The System Prompt Is the Product

blocks.prompt:
The system prompt for Make's coding agent is probably 4,000+ tokens. It defines the agent's behavior, tool catalog, coding conventions, safety rules, and persona. Who owns this document? How do you version it? How do you test changes?

Before reading: think about a 4,000-token document that defines how a product behaves. If a team member edits it without review and ships it, what could go wrong?

---

blocks.content:
#### The System Prompt Is the Product

There is a framing error common in early-stage AI product teams: the system prompt is treated as an engineering configuration file. Someone on the AI team owns it, it lives in a secrets manager or a config repo, and changes get made when the agent does something wrong.

This framing is wrong in a way that creates compounding problems over time.

The system prompt is the agent's behavior specification. Every decision about what the agent does, how it communicates, what it refuses to do, what coding patterns it prefers, what persona it presents — all of it is encoded in the system prompt. That means the system prompt is a product artifact. It encodes PM decisions. It encodes design decisions. It encodes safety decisions. It encodes business logic.

At Figma Make, the system prompt likely specifies: that the agent should prefer Figma's design token naming conventions over generic CSS variable names, that generated components should follow a specific folder structure, that the agent should surface design inconsistencies rather than silently resolve them, that certain operations require user confirmation before execution. These are not engineering preferences. These are product decisions, and they live in the system prompt.

The implication is ownership: the system prompt requires co-ownership between engineering (tool definitions, capability boundaries), product (behavior specifications, user experience of agent responses), and policy/safety (constraint definitions, refusal criteria). No single team should be able to change it unilaterally.

#### Structured Prompt Architecture

An unstructured system prompt grows by accretion. Every time the agent does something unexpected, someone adds a line: "Do not do X." "Always do Y when Z." After a year, the prompt is 8,000 tokens of reactive patches, internally contradictory in places, with no one sure what the original intent was.

Structured prompt architecture prevents this by treating the system prompt like a document with defined sections, each with a purpose and an owner:

**Identity and persona.** Who is the agent? What is its name, its communication style, its relationship to the user? For Make, this section defines that the agent is a collaborator, not an authority — it proposes, the user decides. Tone guidance lives here.

**Capability scope.** What can the agent do? What is explicitly out of scope? This section prevents scope creep in both directions — the agent should not attempt tasks it cannot do reliably, and it should not refuse tasks that are squarely within its capability.

**Tool catalog.** A specification of each available tool: what it does, when to use it, what its parameters are, what its failure modes are. This is the agent's interface to the environment.

**Coding conventions.** Framework preferences, naming patterns, file structure, component organization, import ordering, error handling patterns, test structure. For Figma Make, this section includes design token usage conventions, component library mapping rules, and Figma-specific patterns for handling responsive variants.

**Safety constraints.** What the agent must never do regardless of user instruction. File deletion without confirmation, network requests to external services, writing credentials to files — the hard constraints that exist because the consequences of violation are severe.

**Output format requirements.** How the agent structures its responses: when to write code directly vs. when to explain first, how to surface uncertainty, how to present multiple options, how to format code blocks.

**Error handling instructions.** What the agent does when something goes wrong: how it communicates errors to the user, how it handles ambiguous instructions, what it does when a tool call fails.

This structure is not just organizational. Each section has an owner, a review process, and a change history. When someone wants to change how the agent handles a specific failure mode, they edit the error handling section — they don't add a line to the bottom of the prompt.

#### Prompt Versioning

The system prompt must be version controlled in the same system as the codebase. This is not controversial — it follows directly from the fact that changing the system prompt changes the product's behavior.

What version control gives you:

**Diff history.** When the agent starts behaving unexpectedly, you can look at what changed in the system prompt in the recent past. This is table-stakes for debugging behavior regressions.

**Code review.** Changes to the system prompt should require review — from product, from safety, from engineering, depending on which section is changing. A change to the safety constraints section should require a higher review bar than a change to the coding conventions section.

**Rollback capability.** When a system prompt change causes a regression, you need to be able to revert to the previous version quickly. Without version control, rollback is manual reconstruction.

**Audit trail.** Who changed what and why. This matters for safety reviews, for debugging, and for understanding why the agent behaves the way it does.

The system prompt in the secrets manager or the configuration YAML is the deployed version. It should be generated from the version-controlled source of truth, not edited directly.

#### Prompt Testing: Every Change Is a Behavior Change

This is the most important operational insight in this module: a system prompt change is a model behavior change. It is not a configuration tweak. It is not a documentation update. It changes how the agent responds to every input it receives.

That means prompt changes require the same testing rigor as code changes that ship to users.

The eval suite for the system prompt must cover:

**Canonical tasks.** The core user journeys — generate a component from a Figma frame, fix a build error, add a new route, refactor a component. These should pass before any system prompt change ships.

**Regression cases.** Every significant bug that was fixed via a system prompt edit should become a regression test case. The system prompt change that fixed it should not be the only thing preventing recurrence.

**Edge cases.** Ambiguous instructions, partial information, conflicting requirements — the cases where the agent's behavior is determined by judgment calls encoded in the system prompt. These are the cases most likely to break when the prompt changes.

**Adversarial cases.** Instructions that try to get the agent to violate its safety constraints. The constraints section of the system prompt must be robust to adversarial phrasing.

Running the full eval suite before shipping a system prompt change adds latency to the prompt editing workflow. This is intentional. The latency is the cost of the safety guarantee. An eval suite that takes 20 minutes to run is not a problem to be optimized away — it is a forcing function that prevents casual prompt edits from shipping untested.

This is what Module 4.9 was pointing toward: the eval suite for Make is not just testing code generation quality. It is testing the system prompt's behavioral specification against the full space of inputs the agent will encounter.

#### Prompt Drift and the Rewrite Decision

Prompt drift is the entropy of reactive patching. It follows a predictable pattern:

- The agent does something wrong
- Someone adds a patch: "Never do X when Y"
- The agent does something else wrong
- Someone adds a patch: "Always prefer Z over W"
- Over time, patches accumulate, some contradict each other, and the prompt's internal logic becomes opaque

The accumulation of patches is not just an aesthetics problem. It creates behavioral interference — two instructions that seem unrelated may interact in a specific input context to produce unexpected behavior. The longer the prompt, the more opportunities for interference.

The PM decision is: when does the prompt need a rewrite rather than another patch?

The signal for a rewrite is interference: when a new patch causes a previously-working behavior to break. If fixing the agent's handling of one case breaks its handling of another case, the prompt's internal logic has become incoherent enough that it needs to be rebuilt from principles rather than patched further.

A rewrite is not starting from scratch — it is restructuring the prompt's logic from the current understanding of what the agent should do, expressed in the structured format described above, then reconciling the existing patch history against the new structure to ensure nothing was lost.

#### Prompt A/B Testing

The system prompt can be A/B tested the same way UI features are A/B tested. Two variants of the system prompt — differing in a specific section — are served to different user cohorts. The outcome metrics are the eval suite scores plus any product metrics that are sensitive to agent behavior (session completion rate, number of user corrections, task completion time).

Prompt A/B testing is more complex than UI A/B testing because the treatment effect may be interaction-dependent. Variant B may outperform Variant A on simple tasks but underperform on complex ones. The eval suite needs enough coverage of the task complexity distribution to detect this.

Prompt A/B testing is also how you validate prompt changes that cannot be fully evaluated offline — changes to tone, to the level of explanation the agent provides, to how it surfaces uncertainty. These are dimensions where user behavior in production is a more reliable signal than an offline eval suite.

#### The Prompt-Model Coupling Problem

A system prompt optimized for one model may not work well on another. This is a structural problem for any AI product team that does not control the underlying model.

When Anthropic releases a new Claude version, Figma Make has a decision to make: adopt the new model (potentially better performance, lower cost, new capabilities) or stay on the previous version (known behavior, validated prompts). The cost of adoption is prompt re-evaluation — running the full eval suite against the new model with the existing prompt, identifying regressions, and updating the prompt to restore expected behavior.

This is not a one-time cost. It recurs with every significant model update. Teams that have invested in comprehensive eval suites and structured prompts can execute this re-evaluation efficiently. Teams that have not face a painful choice between missing out on model improvements and shipping untested behavior changes.

The prompt-model coupling problem is also why prompt structure matters for long-term maintainability. A prompt that is internally coherent and section-organized can be systematically re-evaluated section by section against a new model. A patch-accreted prompt with implicit internal logic must be evaluated as a whole, and failures are difficult to attribute to specific sections.

#### Prompt Caching: Architectural Implications

Anthropic's prompt caching feature allows the system prompt prefix to be cached across turns — the model processes the system prompt once and caches the key-value representations, reducing latency and cost for subsequent turns in the same conversation.

The caching behavior has a structural implication: the system prompt should be organized as a static prefix and a dynamic suffix.

The static prefix contains everything that does not change turn-to-turn: identity, capability scope, tool catalog, coding conventions, safety constraints, output format requirements. This prefix is cached and need not be reprocessed on each turn.

The dynamic suffix contains anything that changes per-session or per-user: the current project's specific conventions (injected from memory), the current file manifest, the current task context. This suffix is not cached and is reprocessed on each turn.

The architectural decision is where the static/dynamic boundary falls. Moving content from the dynamic suffix into the static prefix reduces per-turn processing cost but sacrifices the ability to personalize that content per user or per project. Moving content from the static prefix into the dynamic suffix increases personalization but increases cost.

For Make specifically: the generic Figma coding conventions belong in the static prefix (they don't change per user). The user's specific design token naming patterns, their chosen component library, their project's file structure — these belong in the dynamic suffix, injected from the persistent memory system described in Module 4.10.

#### Make-Specific: Prompt as Proprietary IP

The Figma Make system prompt contains proprietary knowledge: how to interpret Figma's design data structures, how to map Figma auto-layout to CSS flexbox/grid, how to handle Figma component instances vs. Figma components, how to use design tokens from Figma Variables in generated code. This is Figma-specific knowledge that is not in any public training dataset in the form the agent needs it.

This means the system prompt is a proprietary competitive asset. It encodes years of understanding about the Figma data model, the patterns of Figma users, and the failure modes specific to design-to-code generation. A competitor building a similar tool must develop this knowledge from scratch. The system prompt is not just a configuration document — it is accumulated institutional knowledge in executable form.

The implication for governance: the system prompt deserves the same protection as core product code. Access controls, audit logging of edits, and review requirements are not bureaucratic overhead — they are protection of a proprietary asset.

---

blocks.question: A user reports that Make's coding agent is generating components with incorrect Figma design token usage — it's using hardcoded hex values instead of design token references. The engineering team wants to patch the system prompt immediately: add a line saying "Always use design tokens from the project's token set; never hardcode color values." Before approving this patch, what do you need to verify?
blocks.answer:
Three things. First, does this patch conflict with any existing instruction in the system prompt? A line like "prioritize readability for developers unfamiliar with design tokens" might create interference with the new constraint. The patch must be evaluated in the context of the full prompt, not in isolation. Second, does the patch have a corresponding eval case? The behavior being fixed should become a regression test: a known Figma frame with design tokens should produce code with design token references, not hex values. Without a test, the same regression can recur. Third, does the patch address the root cause? If the agent is using hardcoded values because the design context schema from the design-reading agent is not passing token mappings through the handoff, a system prompt patch will not fix it — the handoff schema is the actual failure point. The patch must address the mechanism of failure, not just the symptom.

---

blocks.title:
Write a structured system prompt section for a single domain: the coding conventions section for a Make agent that generates React components from Figma files. Include: framework (React with TypeScript), naming conventions, file structure, design token usage rules, component organization, and how to handle Figma component instances that map to existing codebase components.

Keep it under 400 tokens. The constraint is intentional — every word costs at inference time, and clarity under constraint is the skill.

Then identify: which parts of what you wrote belong in the static prefix (unchanging across users) and which belong in the dynamic suffix (injected per-user from memory)?

---

blocks.prompt:
Explain the prompt-model coupling problem to a PM who just joined the team. They're excited about upgrading to the latest Claude model because the benchmark scores are significantly better.

What do you need to do before shipping the model upgrade? What could go wrong if you skip that work? How long might it take, and what determines the answer?

---

questions: What would it look like to design Make's prompt architecture so that a new design system convention is a data change (new memory entry), not a prompt change?
How do you build user trust in an agent whose governing logic is opaque?

What's your instinct on where the transparency/IP protection tradeoff should land for a professional creative tool like Figma?

---

blocks.content:
The system prompt is the agent's behavior specification — a product artifact, not a configuration file — and treating it with the governance discipline of code (version control, structured sections, eval-gated changes, model-specific re-validation) is what separates teams that can iterate safely from teams that ship regressions they cannot explain.

---

blocks.prompt:
You have now covered the full agent infrastructure stack: what agents are, how they use tools, how they manage context, how they reason over multiple steps, how they recover from errors, how they handle deployments, how they fit into design workflows, the economics of their operation, and now — the memory that makes them persistent, the orchestration that makes them scalable, and the prompt architecture that makes them governable.

Level 5 goes up a layer: if all of this infrastructure exists, how do you know if it is working? How do you measure output quality at scale? How do you build the evaluation systems that tell you whether your agent is getting better or worse? That is the eval and measurement problem — and it is where platform PM judgment determines whether agent quality compounds or erodes over time.

reviewQuestions.question: The module argues that the system prompt is a product artifact, not a configuration file. What practical governance change does this framing require?
options: The system prompt requires co-ownership across engineering, product, and safety/policy teams — changes should go through review, and different sections have different review bars depending on what they govern
The system prompt should be stored in the product documentation rather than in a code repository, so non-technical stakeholders can review it
The system prompt should be version controlled only by the AI team, since they are the only team with enough technical context to evaluate its effects
reviewQuestions.explanation: The system prompt encodes PM decisions (what the agent does), design decisions (how it communicates), and safety decisions (what it refuses). No single team should be able to change it unilaterally. Co-ownership means engineering owns tool definitions and capability boundaries, PM owns behavior specifications, and safety/policy owns constraint definitions — each with appropriate review requirements for their section.
reviewQuestions.encourageCorrect: Correct — co-ownership across teams, with differentiated review bars per section, is the governance implication of treating the system prompt as a product artifact.
reviewQuestions.encourageIncorrect: Storing the prompt in product docs reduces auditability and change control. AI-team-only ownership misses the PM and safety decisions embedded in the prompt. The right governance model is co-ownership: engineering, product, and safety each own their relevant sections, and changes to any section require review from the appropriate owner.

reviewQuestions.question: Prompt drift is the entropy that accumulates from reactive patching. What specific technical problem does drift create beyond organizational messiness?
options: Longer prompts consume more tokens per session, increasing API cost proportionally to the number of patches accumulated
Instructions added to fix one behavior can interact with unrelated instructions in unexpected input contexts, creating behavioral interference — fixing one case breaks another
Patches accumulate faster than the model can be retrained, causing the system prompt to become incompatible with newer model versions
reviewQuestions.explanation: Behavioral interference is the technical failure mode: two instructions that seem unrelated may interact in a specific input context to produce unexpected behavior. 'Always use design tokens' combined with an older instruction 'prioritize readability for developers unfamiliar with token names' may produce contradictory behavior on specific prompts. The longer the prompt, the more opportunities for interference — which is why a structured rewrite from principles is sometimes necessary.
reviewQuestions.encourageCorrect: Right — behavioral interference from instruction interaction is the specific technical problem that makes prompt drift more than just an aesthetics issue.
reviewQuestions.encourageIncorrect: Token cost scales with prompt length but isn't the core technical risk. Model compatibility is a separate concern. The technical problem with prompt drift is behavioral interference: two patches that seem unrelated can interact on specific inputs to produce contradictory behavior. This makes debugging prompt failures difficult because the interaction is hard to trace.

reviewQuestions.question: The prompt-model coupling problem means that adopting a new Claude model version requires prompt re-evaluation. What is the minimum work required to do this responsibly?
options: Read the new model's release notes to understand capability changes, then ship the model upgrade if no breaking changes are mentioned
Deploy the new model to 1% of users for 24 hours and monitor customer support tickets for complaints about agent behavior changes
Run the full eval suite against the new model with the existing system prompt, identify behavioral regressions, and update the prompt to restore expected behavior before shipping
reviewQuestions.explanation: A prompt optimized for one model may not produce the same behavior on another — even if the new model is nominally 'better' on benchmarks. Running the full eval suite against the new model identifies where behavior changes, and prompt updates restore expected behavior before any user sees the regression. Reading release notes is insufficient; 1% rollout without an eval suite means users discover regressions first.
reviewQuestions.encourageCorrect: Exactly — the eval suite is the mechanism for detecting model-induced behavioral regressions before they reach users.
reviewQuestions.encourageIncorrect: Release notes describe intended changes, not actual behavioral regressions in your specific prompt configuration. A 1% rollout without an eval suite means real users encounter the regressions and report them via support tickets — which is a poor substitute for catching them before any rollout. The correct minimum is running the full eval suite against the new model and fixing regressions in the prompt before shipping.
